glossary.def: A family of methods that learn values or policies directly from sampled experience without ever building or being given the environment's transition and reward model. The expectation that dynamic programming computes by summing against a known model is instead recovered by averaging actual transitions (s, a, r, s′).

Estimating a value function by averaging the actual returns that follow each state under a fixed policy. It is unbiased and makes no Markov assumption, but must wait until an episode terminates to compute the return and tends to have high variance.

Updating a value estimate using another current estimate rather than a fully observed outcome — the defining trick of temporal-difference learning, which replaces the unknown future return G_{t+1} with the agent's own guess V(s_{t+1}). It enables online, single-step learning even in tasks that never terminate, at the cost of introducing bias.

The quantity δ_t = R_{t+1} + γ V(s_{t+1}) − V(s_t): the gap between two successive predictions of the same return, before and after seeing one step of reward. Driving it toward zero is the engine of nearly every value method in modern RL, and a strikingly similar signal is encoded by dopamine neurons in the brain.

An off-policy TD control algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function Q* by bootstrapping from the greedy next action, max_{a′} Q(s′, a′), regardless of how the agent actually behaves. It converges to Q* with probability 1 in finite MDPs under standard conditions, but the max operator induces a maximization bias that overestimates values.

A method is on-policy if it learns about the same policy it uses to act, and off-policy if it can learn about one policy while behaving according to another. SARSA is on-policy (it bootstraps from the action it actually takes, learning the value of its own exploratory behavior); Q-learning is off-policy (it bootstraps from the greedy action, learning the optimal policy it does not follow).

An action-selection rule that takes the current greedy (best-estimated) action with probability 1−ε and a uniformly random action with probability ε. ε is usually annealed from high to low under a GLIE schedule — greedy in the limit with infinite exploration — so the agent explores widely early and exploits what it has learned later while keeping a small permanent exploration floor.

refs.cite: Watkins, C. J. C. H. & Dayan, P. (1992). Q-learning. Machine Learning 8.
Sutton, R. S. (1988). Learning to Predict by the Methods of Temporal Differences. Machine Learning 3.
Sutton, R. S. & Barto, A. G. (2018). Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (2nd ed.). MIT Press.
Mnih, V. et al. (2015). Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning. Nature 518.
van Hasselt, H. (2010). Double Q-learning. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 23.
Singh, S., Jaakkola, T., Littman, M. L. & Szepesvári, C. (2000). Convergence Results for Single-Step On-Policy Reinforcement-Learning Algorithms. Machine Learning 38.
